title: Booleans (True/False) ⚖️
description: Understand logic with true and false values.

slides.title: Yes or No? 🚦
slides.content:
# The Boolean Data Type

Sometimes you only need to store a simple 'Yes' or 'No', 'On' or 'Off'.

In programming, we call these **Booleans**.

Ruby has exactly two boolean values: `true` and `false`.

They do NOT need quotation marks.

slides.title: Using Booleans
slides.content: We can assign `true` and `false` to variables just like numbers and strings.
slides.codeSnippet:
is_sunny = true
is_raining = false

puts is_sunny
puts is_raining

quizData.question: What is the difference between `true` and `"true"`?

options.text: `true` is a boolean, while `"true"` is a string.

quizData.explanation: Quotation marks make it a string (just text). Without quotes, Ruby recognizes `true` as the special built-in boolean value.

slides.title: Comparing Values ⚖️
slides.content:
You will rarely manually type `true` or `false`. Instead, computers generate booleans by answering math or logic questions!

You can ask Ruby to compare values using these operators:

* `>` (Greater than)
* `<` (Less than)
* `==` (Is exactly equal to?)
* `!=` (Is NOT equal to?)

slides.title: Generating Booleans
slides.content: Run the code. The result of these comparisons will ALWAYS be `true` or `false`.

quizData.question: What will `puts 10 == 9` output to the screen?
quizData.explanation: Because 10 is NOT equal to 9, the statement is false.

slides.title: One Equals vs Two Equals
slides.content:
A single `=` **assigns** a value to a variable.
Double `==` **compares** two values to see if they are the exact same.
Don't mix them up!
